after_heading: 1. The Foundation-Model Shift: Pretrain Once, Forecast Anywhere
prompt: A witty, cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a calm wise owl scholar stands at the shore of a vast glowing ocean made of countless overlapping wavy time-series lines stretching to the horizon, having clearly drunk deep from that sea, and now it confidently turns to read a single small unfamiliar wavy ribbon a nervous visitor holds out, sketching the ribbon's continuation in the air with a feather pen without any books or study. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry, knowing, and immediately readable: a reader who just learned this concept should smile in recognition. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A wise owl that has drunk from a vast ocean of overlapping wavy time-series curves now reads a single unfamiliar wavy ribbon held out by a nervous visitor and sketches its continuation in the air, dramatizing a model pretrained on a sea of diverse series then applied cold to a brand-new one it has never met.
caption: Pretrain once on a sea of diverse series, then forecast a stranger you have never met, no fitting required.

prompt: A witty, cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a row of plump round data-point pebbles of varying heights are fed one by one into a friendly slicing machine that snaps each into a discrete colored token-block from a small shelf of bins, and the resulting line of blocks marches off arm-in-arm like a string of cheerful word-creatures pretending to be a spoken sentence, while a language-model robot happily reads them as if they were speech. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. alt: Continuous data-point pebbles pass through a slicing machine that quantizes each into a discrete colored token-block, and the blocks march off arm-in-arm like word-creatures forming a sentence that a language-model robot reads, dramatizing how Chronos quantizes a real-valued series into discrete tokens so a language model can forecast it.
caption: Quantize a number series into discrete tokens and it can masquerade as a sentence, which is exactly how Chronos lets a language model forecast it.

after_heading: 2. TimesFM: A Decoder-Only Patched Foundation Model
prompt: A witty, cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a long wavy ribbon of history is sliced into neat equal tiles that ride a conveyor belt into a sturdy decoder engine-house, which after each tile it swallows extrudes a fresh tile of future ribbon out the far chute, the new tile immediately looping back onto the belt to be eaten again, a tidy self-feeding forecasting factory built from web-scale traffic curves piled in the background. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry, knowing, and immediately readable: a reader who just learned this concept should smile in recognition. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A history ribbon is sliced into equal patch-tiles that feed a decoder engine-house which extrudes future tiles that loop back onto the belt, dramatizing how the decoder-only patched TimesFM consumes patches of a series and autoregressively emits the next patches, pretrained on web-scale traffic curves.
caption: Slice the past into patches, feed them to a decoder, and let each forecast patch loop back as input: TimesFM's pretrain-at-scale forecasting engine in one picture.

caption: A pocket-sized model can match giants it has no business matching: the Tiny Time Mixer punches far above its parameter count, zero-shot and cheap.

after_heading: 1. The Surprising Idea: Forecasting by Completing a String of Digits
prompt: A witty, cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a bookish word-loving language-model character surrounded by floating speech bubbles and a quill squints skeptically through tiny reading glasses at an unfamiliar wavy forecast curve handed to it, clearly never trained for this, scratching its head, and yet from its quill flows a continuation of the curve that lands surprisingly close to the true path, the character itself looking baffled that its guess worked. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry, knowing, and immediately readable: a reader who just learned this concept should smile in recognition. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A bookish language-model character squints through reading glasses at an unfamiliar wavy forecast curve it was never trained for, scratches its head, yet its quill draws a continuation close to the true path while it looks baffled, dramatizing how a frozen general-purpose LLM forecasts a numeric series surprisingly well by simply continuing it.
caption: A wordy language model squints at a chart it was never trained for and somehow guesses the continuation well, the unsettling surprise behind LLM-based forecasting.

after_heading: 1. Evaluation Protocols That Matter for Foundation Models

alt: A relaxed forecaster strolls out of an exam hall holding a paper full of checkmarks despite a bare study desk with no notes, while a classmate who crammed with a tower of books looks on dumbfounded, dramatizing zero-shot evaluation where a pretrained model scores well on a held-out series it never trained on.
caption: Zero-shot forecasting is acing an exam you never studied for, and the honest evaluation question is whether the crammer beside you still scored higher.
